column2: The Hypertext Transfer Protocol, a stateless request/response protocol used to exchange web resources between clients and servers.
HTTP layered over TLS, so requests and responses are encrypted and the server's identity is authenticated.
A Uniform Resource Locator: the address of a resource, giving its scheme, host, optional port, path, and query.
A Uniform Resource Identifier: a string that identifies a resource; a URL is a URI that also locates it.
Representational State Transfer, an architectural style that models operations as HTTP methods acting on addressable resources.
JavaScript Object Notation, a lightweight text format for structured data built from objects, arrays, numbers, strings, booleans, and null.
An Application Programming Interface: a defined contract through which one program requests services from another.
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, a browser mechanism that lets a server opt in to requests from web pages of a different origin.
A small piece of data a server asks a browser to store and send back on later requests, commonly used for sessions.
The Transmission Control Protocol, a connection-oriented transport that provides a reliable, ordered byte stream with flow and congestion control.
The User Datagram Protocol, a connectionless transport that sends best-effort datagrams with no delivery or ordering guarantees.
The Internet Protocol, the network-layer protocol that addresses hosts and routes packets between networks.
A 16-bit number that identifies a specific service or endpoint on a host, so multiple connections can share one IP address.
An endpoint of a network connection, identified by an IP address and port, through which a program sends and receives data.
A formatted unit of data carried by a packet-switched network, consisting of control headers and a payload.
The Domain Name System, a hierarchical distributed directory that resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.
The Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol, which automatically assigns IP addresses and network settings to hosts on a network.
A Media Access Control address, a hardware identifier assigned to a network interface for communication on a local link.
Transport Layer Security, a protocol that provides encryption, integrity, and server authentication for network connections.
A system that monitors and filters network traffic according to configured rules, allowing or blocking flows at a boundary.
A Virtual Private Network, which tunnels traffic over a shared network inside an encrypted, authenticated connection.
An intermediary server that forwards requests and responses between clients and origin servers, often adding caching or policy.
Network Address Translation, which rewrites addresses in packet headers so many private hosts can share one public IP address.
A subdivision of an IP network, defined by a prefix length, that groups hosts sharing a common routing prefix.
A node that connects two networks and forwards traffic between them, typically the router a host uses to reach other networks.
A Content Delivery Network, a geographically distributed set of caching servers that serve content from close to the user.
A component that distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers to improve capacity and availability.
The time it takes for a unit of data to travel from source to destination across a network.
The maximum rate at which data can be transferred across a network path, usually measured in bits per second.
